senses.definition: plural of smorgasbord, a large mixed meal with many different foods displayed buffet-style.
examples: There were several tables of dishes at the party, a real smorgasbord.
The hotel offered a smorgasbord of breakfast options.

senses.definition: informal abbreviation for smorgasbords; multiple types of foods laid out in a buffet.
examples: The restaurant offers some classic smorgs for travelers.
We sampled a few smorgs before choosing our dinner.

senses.definition: present participle of smor, though this form is not standard in modern English; likely a misspelling or nonce form related to smore or smoulder; no widely recognized sense in standard usage.
examples: This entry does not have a standard sense in contemporary English; usage may be idiosyncratic or erroneous.

senses.definition: a Danish open-faced sandwich consisting of a slice of rye bread topped with various ingredients.
examples: Smorrebrod is a traditional Danish lunch.
The cafe served an assortment of smorrebrod with different toppings.

senses.definition: plural of smorrebrod; multiple Danish open-faced sandwiches.
examples: The platter contained several smorrebrods.
A variety of smorrebrods were available for tasting.

senses.definition: a musical direction meaning gradually dying away in speed and intensity; becoming softer and slower.
examples: The conductor marked the passage smorzando to lead into the quiet ending.
Play the section smorzando, letting the notes fade gently.

senses.definition: a musical direction meaning slightly more lively than smorzando; gradually faster or more animated while still soft.
examples: The piece transitions to smorzato before the final cadence.
She played with a smorzato character, keeping the tone gentle.

senses.definition: past tense of smite; struck with a weapon or strong blow; defeated or conquered.
examples: The knight smote the dragon with a mighty sword.
In the debate, he was smote by a sharp counterargument.

senses.definition: to cover someone or something completely to prevent air or light from reaching; to suffocate; to suppress or stifle.
examples: She smothered the candle with a lid to quiet the flame.
The crowd tried not to smother the small child with hugs.
He smothered his excitement when the news arrived.

senses.definition: capable of being smothered; able to be covered or suppressed.
examples: The fire was small and easily smotherable with a cover.
Some herbs are smotherable by heavy sauces.

senses.definition: the act or result of smothering; suffocation or suppression.
examples: The fire’s smotheration cut off the oxygen supply.
Smotheration of dissent can harm a healthy discussion.

senses.definition: covered or suppressing something; having been suffocated or overwhelmed.
examples: The cake was smothered in chocolate.
Smothered in shame, she avoided eye contact.

senses.definition: a person or thing that smothers; one who suffocates or suppresses.
examples: The smotherer of dissent is not welcome here.
The blanket acted as a smotherer on the campfire.

senses.definition: plural of smotherer; people or things that smother.
examples: The protest was stifled by several smotherers in positions of authority.

senses.definition: the quality of being smothering or overly suffocating; heaviness of mood or atmosphere.
examples: The smotheriness of the air made the room feel oppressive.
We avoided the smotheriness of the crowd by stepping outside.

senses.definition: plural of smotheriness; multiple instances of smotheriness.
examples: The essay explored several smotherinesses in urban life.

senses.definition: present participle or gerund of smother; covering completely or suppressing.
examples: Smothering heat made the room uncomfortable.
Smothering relief washed over him when the test ended.

senses.definition: in a way that smothers or overpowers; suffocatingly.
examples: The room smelled smotheringly of damp wool.
Smotheringly dense fog lay over the city.

senses.definition: plural of smothering; acts or instances of smothering.
examples: The smotherings of his gestures confused the audience.

senses.definition: third-person singular present of smother; covers or suppresses something or someone.
examples: He smothers the flame with a lid.
Crowds smothered the exits, making it hard to move.

senses.definition: characterized by smothering conditions; suffocating or oppressive.
examples: The attic was smothery and hot in the afternoon.

senses.definition: one who smites; a person who strikes, especially in history or myth; not a common modern usage.
examples: The old epic speaks of the smotter who toppled giants.

senses.definition: a quick, casual kiss or a light, soft touch; in some dialects, a playful squeeze.
examples: Give him a quick smouch on the cheek.
They shared a cheeky smouch before running off.

senses.definition: past tense of smouch; kissed or touched lightly in a casual way.
examples: She smouched him on the cheek and laughed.

senses.definition: a person who smouches; a light, quick kiss or a sweet bite; also a slang for a cigarette lighter or lighter in some regions.
examples: The couple shared a playful smoucher in the hallway.
In some places, a smoucher means a small kiss.
